It will do so by providing guidance to coders on how to assign Type 1, 2, 3 or 4 status to qualitative text including, but not limited to, peer reviewed journal articles, course syllabi, policy statements, policy analyses, media communications and policy recommendations. This classification effort complements, rather than replaces, careful qualitative application of the framework required for uncovering important nuances through deliberative analysis and reflection of highly complex text. ***What the quantitative coding effort does well is to generate useful knowledge about whether the overall patterns identified by an one study or set of studies compare to other cases outside of the study. Coding also permits assessments as to whether there are differences in the dominance of one or more problem types across time and also space – be they countries, organizations, or sectors.*** Hence, not only can a well-developed code book generate descriptions about a range of policy and governance outcomes, it will also allow for broadening epistemological and methodological techniques that might explain variation – such as the correlation between the kind of disciplinary, sub-disciplinary and interdisciplinary training experts received that shape their bias in emphasizing one Type over the other. This in turn, might help generate knowledge about, and reverse when desired, “unconscious bias” about the way policy scholars and practitioners conceive of, and measure, “on the ground” problems. In other words, this framework can help advance “fit for purpose” policy analysis. To be clear: unlike Cashore’s broader argument about the drift away from Type 4 problems that he has, and is, applying to understand their role in shaping sustainability policy and governance over the last 35 years, the code book is designed to describe a range of outcomes, to which Cashore and his collaborators, and anyone else for that matter, can better assess, test, and develop their own arguments and theories. In short, the code book, paves the way for, but does not take a position on, which of the four problem types is preferred nor the causes of changes in emphasis of the Types. Importantly, although the 4PT distinctions originally came out of Cashore’s work on global, transnational and local approaches to sustainability policy and governance, they can be applied to disentangle how any kind of problem facing society and policy making are analysed, and treated, by scholars and experts. This means that ***while Cashore applies and draws examples from sustainability policy scholarship and practice, the framework is equally useful for anyone working on problem focused policy analysis including, but not limited to those scholars working in schools of public health, education, social work, forestry, fisheries, natural resources, agriculture, and even mining and energy for that matter.*** Having said this, the guidance and examples below for coders to be able to render concrete important but sometimes abstract distinctions, draw on examples from five years of efforts to code sustainability policy and governance analyses. This code book proceeds in the following steps. First, we discuss Cashore’s “utility” and “problem solving” labels as the two key distinctions that position the 4PT framework. Second, we review Cashore’s four key schools of sustainability thought and practice – including notable strands within them – to show how each of these 4PTs are subtly nurtured by sophisticated scholarly communities. Third, we review the challenges facing coding of different kinds of qualitative documents. Fourth, we review different sorting challenges based on the kinds of document being including abstracts of course syllabi, journal articles. Fifth we offer solutions to each including reviewing of our protocol for sorting scholarly articles through careful qualitative analysis that has been developed and revised over the last five years. We then review our approach to coding course syllabi and the development of key words to facilitate swift coding of a range of documents. We then discuss our systematic efforts to advance AI assisted coding with the goal of producing systematic text analysis that would be consistent with the laborious human coding efforts of the last six years (i.e. we seek to achieve intercoder reliability between AI and humans .8 or better). We conclude by offering recommendations on next steps for both Cashore’s team and collaborators, as well as those who might want to apply the 4PT framework beyond sustainability policy and governance considerations. Appendices A reviews our approach to coding articles beyond the two distinctions as a way to generate propositions about preliminary explanations for results and Appendix B which provides examples from cases with which to assist in sorting initially “ambiguous” cases. Section 2: ## a) Key Distinctions: Utility and Problem Solving Orientations The specific coding task appears at first glance seems quite straightforward i.e. to distinguish whether scholarly and practitioner policy analyses emphasise, or not, “utility” and “problem solving” orientations (Table 1). | Table1: The Four Problem Types (4PT)
Simplified Distinctions | Utility Dominates? YES | Utility Dominates? No | | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------- | | **Problem Solving Dominates? YES** | TYPE 1 | TYPE 4 | | **Problem Solving Dominates? NO** | TYPE 2 | TYPE 3 | These two distinctions originally emerged from Cashore’s reflections, individually and with colleagues, about why so many sustainability policy experts have disagreed for over 35 years about how to think about, and also manage, critical environmental challenges facing the planet highlighted by climate, biodiversity and forest degradation. (Again, while derived from the sustainability policy and governance cases, the 4PT distinctions are meant to be applied to any “on the ground” problems facing policy makers and society including health care, education, child care, education and so on). The origins of the 4PT distinctions followed Cashore’s recognition, having only worked in interdisciplinary forestry, environment and policy schools, that longstanding largely unconscious biases among himself, his colleagues, and experts generally, about how to study the world of sustainability policy and governance, helped explain longstanding debates about not only about how to conceive of, and measure, sustainability challenges, but also recommended courses of policy action for managing the environment, including climate, species extinctions, biodiversity loss, as well as forests and natural resources management. What Cashore noticed was two key divides within these debates. ***The first distinction*** was whether sustainability scholars and practitioners believed that enhancing **“utility”** outcomes was the key task facing sustainability policy analysis. He noted that a large group of scholars generally felt that the more utility was enhanced, the better off society will be. While this causal belief is widely diffused among a range of analysts, it is also hotly debated by scholars and analysts who either oppose utility as a guiding orientation, and/or seek other ways to frame desired outcomes. Hence, Cashore recognized that the belief that utility enhancement was the preferable, from those that did not, was an excellent way to distinguish sustainability policy and governance analyses. ***Cashore’s second distinction*** came from his recognition that while some narrowed their conclusion to assessing how to solve or effectively manage the clearly specified problem at hand- such as such as saving a particular species from extinction, avoiding resource collapse of a specified fish stock, or limiting warming to 1.5 degrees above pre-industrial levels – others brought in analytical frames that allowed for a deliberations over not only how, but ***whether*** the problem at hand could or should be solved. That is, this latter group applied adjudicating frameworks that were developed as a way to address ***any*** particular problem – regardless as to its unique features - that might find its way to the societal or policy agenda in mind. Rather than generating research and analysis to shed light on how to manage a specific policy challenge, this group was united by developing frameworks that allow for consideration of not acting at all, or acting in a way that does not effectively manage the problem at hand. Section 3: ## b) Conceptual Foundations: Utility and Problem Contingent Labels While these distinctions showed great promise, the next task was to elaborate their meanings so that their influence could be better identified and assessed. Section 4: ### The utility label covers a broad range Cashore’s 4PT framework notes that the term “utility” is generally applied and defined broadly. It includes for example, those trained in welfare economics who, largely focusing on income or economic growth, convert others outcomes to “utility equivalents” in order to monetize, and compare, human preferences for green space and nature, with their direct interest in generating income. The term “utility” also incorporates the broader philosophy of “utilitarianism” that seeks to advance outcomes that produce the “greatest good for the greatest number in the long run”. It is also used more generally as a term to discuss whether, in terms of enhancing individual or collection satisfactions, there is any “utility” in a proposed course of action? For Cashore, the rationale for use of “utility” as a label was that it best captures the general belief behind a range of analyses that their goal is to improve “**human satisfactions”.** This is an important clarification because the utility as a term has a decidedly anthropogenetic orientation in which policy is geared towards improving the interests of humans either today or in the future. This, in turn, is shaped by some kind of homage and widespread work developing theories relevant to unpacking human **“self-interest”** .What is important for coding on the 4PT framework is that the concern itself is what is important – i.e. **the utility label in the 4PT framework is shorthand to capture those who are motivated to advance human self-interested satisfactions that incorporate a range of more specific satisfaction seeking rationales** This orientation in turn, allows coders to identify efforts to promote and understand **self-interested behaviours at multiple levels** of sustainability policy and governance including individual, organizational, nation state decisions as well as those within international governance arenas and global governance agreements. Hence, the utility label includes, for coding purposes, analyses focused on aggregating **individual utility** , **organizational utility** (such as companies returning shareholder value), or even **states’ utility** by maximizing their own self-interested preferences. This category incorporates, rather than distinguishes, longstanding debates among neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars within international relations about whether self-interested, utility chasing states, are motivated to achieve relative or absolute gains. (The 4PT framework notes that debates about relative or absolute gains have undermine the similar quest to advance utility seeking interests, including, at the state level, relying on some conception of “national interest” that improves the satisfaction of its population through some kind of beneficial “rational” outcome. **Hence, this broad use of the utility label focuses coders on identifying any kind of analysis that is motivated by enhancing human satisfaction through self-interested preference maximization** . This allows coders to capture in one category a range of scholarship and analysis that although diverse and nuanced, are united by their underlying orientation in emphasizing individual, organizational and state based preferences that pay homage to improving human “satisfactions” at multiple levels. Section 5: ### The problem solving label: distinguishing analyses that target “how”, not “whether” --- The term “problem solving” is also used broadly to capture a range of expertise and approaches that undertake research with which to solve or effectively manage a particular “on the ground” problem. That is, they eschew asking “whether” a problem can be effectively managed, and instead ask, given the problem at hand, how can it be effectively managed? This category then incorporates a broad group of scholars and analysts who are united in their quest to understand and effectively management an “on the ground” problem such as the extinction of a particular species or limiting warming to no more than 1.5/2 °C above pre-industrial levels promoted through the Paris Accord. This means that the analyses they bring, and conclusions they make, will go back to implications for generating knowledge useful for problem solving of a problem, or a class of problems. Similarly, these analysts will never apply universal pre-established theories to assess whether the problem at hand ***ought*** to be solved. This is not their interest. Likewise while their research may carry lessons for other problems, they do not seek to generate universal theories that can be applied to any kind of problem. Put another way, this group of scholars would, for these reasons, avoid any quest for developing a theoretical orientation, or set of recommendations, that went beyond the problem or class of problems from which their research was derived. The practical result is that many in this category tend to carefully assess problem structure or “key features of the problem” they are attempting to shed light on how to better manage or solve. This is important. The reason they eschew any effort to develop a universal way to address any problem is because, by disconnecting from problem structure, the approach applied elsewhere might not effectively manage or solve another class of problems – i.e. it might even make those worse off. This means that they are largely conducting research in which its lessons are “problem contingent”. Any effort to generate lessons beyond a class of problems that is offered as a way to think about, and manage, all problems, is evidence that a scholarly analysis is not in this category. More specifically, any effort that concludes it is not “feasible” or “rational” to address a particular problem at hand that draws on a pre-established theory or framework that that was developed to analyse any and all problem is outside of this sorting category. This elaboration leads to the identification of a more nuanced table of which human and AI coders must be aware. Coders will note that I have also changed the Y axis label to “problem contingent” which I treat as synonymous as “problem solving” dominates, but also more useful for coding purposes. I keep the broader term in the first table as it is more accessible to the uninitiated coder, while bringing in the more specific term once they have read these first few overview pages. | Table 2: The Four Problem Types
Expanded Distinctions | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? YES | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? NO | | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? YES** | TYPE 1 Utility / Contingent | TYPE 4 Not Utility / Contingent | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? NO** | TYPE 2 Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | TYPE 3 Not Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | In sum, at the risk of repetition, let me be clear: these frameworks offer ways to deliberate over ***not only how, but whether, a specific problem can be effectively managed given a broader set of considerations beyond the problem*** itself. Section 6: ## C) Examples of Not Problem Contingent (Universal Approaches) As I discuss below I refer to the major strand of Optimization school of sustainability thought and practice known as “cost-benefit analysis” (CBA) as a leading example of this approach. This is because CBA frameworks assesses whether or not a policy intervention governing a particular problem will be able to produce, or not, aggregate utility benefits. Under this approach, if policy designers cannot find an intervention to address a problem at hand that will enhance overall utility (i.e. possible policy interventions are all deemed to undermine aggregate utility) then the “rational” answer from this CBA approach is not to act. There are, to be sure, many other strands of the Optimization school. For example, within international relations, both neo-realist and neo-liberal international relations scholars seek to generate theories of state preference, including longstanding debates about whether states maximize their preferences owing to absolute or relative gains. What is important for the 4PT framework is not their debates about underlying motivations that shape a given state’s “self-interest” preferences, but that both neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars develop their theories and frameworks to explain ***state behaviour in general*** – i.e. they seek universal understandings of utility chasing state preference that explain, but are not derived from, their choices about how to manage a specified problem. That is, they develop and apply theories of state preferences that apply beyond a particular problem, but are meant to explain state behaviour over a particular problem. To repeat, their project is to develop universal theories of state action that are not derived from, or limited to, any specific problem or issue states happen to be deliberating over. These theories of state action also inform recommendations from these scholar about how states might act go address a specific problem in order to better achieve their pre-existing self-interested preferences (i.e. how to “optimize” outcomes given existing understandings of state preferences). This is why, as I discuss below, so many IR scholars make the case for addressing the global climate crisis based on the argument that doing so will advance state preferences, rather than asking whether a theory of state preferences is the most useful way to think about, and manage, particular kinds of problems. This same dilemma facing cost-benefit scholars who so believe in the utility enhancing motivations that they constantly argue, and work so hard to demonstrate – even despite overwhelming evidence to the contrary – that an emphasis on the utility undermining effects of the climate crisis is the most useful way to conceive of, and develop policy options for, averting global warming. As I discuss below, strands within the “Compromise School” of sustainability thought and practice are also replete with a range of efforts to develop universal theories of stakeholder engagement, legitimacy formation, and conflict resolution that, while rejecting utility chasing theories, nonetheless also develop frameworks for understanding motivations of individuals, organizations and states, that are also developed to understand, and prescribe solutions for, a range of problems that may come across the desk of policy officials. For example, there is one strand of the Compromise school known as “alternative dispute resolution” ADR that offers a way for stakeholder to agree on litigation avoiding natural resources management agreements that draws on universal theories of individual and organization behaviour as a way foster a compromise agreement among disparate interests. While drawing on theories that extend far beyond, and reject, utility motivations as far to narrow an approach to human behaviour, they share the same mission in developing university theories as a way to generate an understanding about not only how, but whether, the “on the ground” issue, or set of issues, can be resolved given the overall focus on conflict resolution rather than solving and effectively managing, the “on the ground” problem that spark the ADR in the first place. Another stand of the compromise school has been nurtured by those studying philosophy which seeks to generate a set of norms and protocols for making difficult decisions – such as whether to save someone stopped on the train tracks that might risk the lives of many on the train. In these cases, pre-existing approaches for developing normative theory based protocols for doing so – not based on utility per se but on claims to higher level “ethics” are turned to as a way to have society and policy makers thinks about managing difficult questions – from species extinctions that results from enhancing economic growth – to saving lives during a pandemic when the isolationist policies for doing say may undermine children’s cognitive development. In these cases what is important for the 4PT framework is that the adjudicating analysis that result are meant to help policy makers and society make decisions to address any problem that might come along, and are not derived, narrowed to, or contingent, on a particular class of problems. Section 7: ## d) Examples of Problem Contingent Approaches (Not Universal) In contrast, and as I elaborate below, members of Commons and Prioritization schools narrowing that adjudicating frameworks to only address, and are indeed derived from, a particular “on the ground” problem or class of problems – whether it be for utility enhancing (Commons) or non-utility (Prioritization) motivations. There are many strands within each of these schools that reinforce “problem contingent” rather than non-problem contingent universal (i.e.. meant to be applied to any problems) frameworks. As I discuss below the most famous utility seeking, problem contingent strand within sustainability studies are those seeking to understand, and effectively manage, “tragedies of the commons” that, if not well managed, produce avoidable resource collapse. Here, scholars seek to understand key features of the problem – such as Ostrom’s fundamental point that some commons tragedies – such as fish that swim - cannot be served by “excluding” access – and hence require different solutions to effectively manage them. The point for the “problem contingent” classification here is that scholars like Ostrom and her students who focus on “non-excludable” tragedies, only develop theories and approaches based on these key features. That is, they do not research, and have little to say, problems that do not share these key features. There are many likeminded strands about developing problem contingent approaches by those who do not share the Common’s utility orientation, but do share is focus on how to effectively manage specified “on the ground” problems – i.e. they eschew considerations about “whether” a specified problem can be solved, to deliberating over how it can solved or effectively managed. For example, Tribe’s work on species extinctions rejected analytical universal approaches offered by extant “policy sciences” framework, to show how any consideration to saving a particular species must begin, and end, based on the knowledge about what kind of conservation efforts is required to maintain the viability of that species. While Tribe and others like him turned to debates in philosophy and the study of “ethics”, he rejected as problematic any effort to address species extinctions that might produce an outcome that caused species extinctions. Hence universal adjudicating frames about particular protocols for assessing policy dilemmas – based on universal theories of “ethics”, was in fact, highly unethical. Others in this line of thinking within international relations have pointed to the “on the ground” problem of slavery. For this group, like species extinctions, the only way to eradicate slavery to develop policies and interventions based on achieving no-slavery. The idea of applying cost-benefitanalysis, or alternative dispute resolution, to assessing not only how, but whether, slavery can be eradicated is anathema to this strands of the prioritization school because it opens the door to consider policy options in which slavery might continue. Hence for these scholars, adjudicating frameworks are always derived from knowledge to manage a specified problems (or class of problems with similar structural features and attributes, such as Ostrom’s non-excludability tragedies). These distinctions have led Cashore to elaborate four categories to include these broader distinctions (Table 2) in which we can now further narrow “problem solving” to “problem contingent” which more accurately labels theories and frameworks and course of action that are never meant to be universal, but only applicable to a class of problems Section 8: ## e) Examples of the Resulting Four Problem Types (4PT) Now that we have covered how the 4PT framework defines utility and problem solving distinctions, and reviewed examples of universal versus problem contingent approaches, we are now in a position to identify examples of approaches classified within each: Type 1 (utility, problem contingent); Type 2 (utility, not problem contingent); Type 3 (not utility, not problem contingent); Type 4 (not utility, problem contingent). This section will, out of necessity, repeat some of the examples above. This is by design: repetition of examples will provide clarity when the same ones are used to distinguish different parts of the classification framework. In addition, this part is important since the coding is focused on the resulting four distinctions – so understanding how the above review figures for making decision about where to place a range of literatures is greatly facilitated by giving these concrete examples. This review, in turn, paves the way for a broader discussion of four key schools of sustainability that reinforce, but are also much broader, than the 4PTs. While broader, inclusion of discussion of the schools is expected to generate knowledge that will facilitate and enhance and generate a nuanced approach to coding the 4PTs. Section 9: ### i. Type 1: Utility Dominates, Problem Contingent **Type 1** then sorts those that are motivated by utility chasing orientations, but narrow their focus to a specified “on the ground” problem that is seen as undermining utility enhancement. Those scholars that are sorted into this category seek to generate analyses and ideas for intervening that improve utility outcomes for that problem. These approaches target the problem or class of problems they seek to ameliorate. Their interest is not in developing universal theories to address any and all sustainability problems. This means that they generate recommendations and analyses based on their problem or class of problems in isolation from other problems that might have different features. And they certainly would never apply a theoretical or analytical framework to discuss whether the problem should be solved. There are countless examples of this kind of orientation. For example, I have a colleague who studies the issue of taxis idling looking for riders in downtown Singapore when it appears that they would make more income (utility) by driving to the airport. This problem then can be solved by reducing “information asymmetry” though some kind of intervention – such as advertising or sharing this knowledge, or through some kind of regulation reducing an ability to idle. In this case, interventions are adjudicated only for their ability to manage that particular problem - regardless as to the possible effects of the “effective policy intervention” in reducing overall aggregate utility – such as those case in which limited government resources are better placed on managing another problem that would lead to higher utility. As I discussed above and elaborate below Type 1 sorting would also include a large group of scholars and practitioners working on “collective action theory” in general, and its application to sustainability through the “tragedy of the commons metaphor” in particular. This is an important distinction. Collective Action theorists are motivate by eliminating utility undermining outcomes such as rubber necking on highways, hording of toilet paper during pandemics, or over harvesting that leads to collapse of fish stocks or water supplies. These approaches, which draw on the classic “prisoners dilemma” in which, in the absence of an ability to develop and comply with, a collective agreements, two prisoners in different cells both have a “self-interest” to confess because if they do not, but their partner in crime does, they will be handed a much higher and longer prison sentence. This “confessing/confessing” strategy is considered to the most effective utility enhancing approach given no collective agreement (an outcome known as the “Nash equilibrium) but it is not the most utility enhancing solution. The most utility enhancing solution is for both not to confess, as this will lead to lower prison sentences overall. In this case, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions to allow for both not to confess. In the sustainability world, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions such that harvesting of the resource in question is less than or equal to the biological rate or reproduction. In all these cases, analyses are focused on human satisfactions, but only around the specific problem in question. As I discussed above and in more detail below, Elinor Ostrom’s path to earning the Nobel prize in economics was sparked by her recognition that for some utility undermining tragedies, highlighted by fish, excludability was not possible. This mean that traditional interventions to solve this utility undermining commons tragedies that turned to private property or state ownership, and that assumed excludability was possible, were ill suited to addressing those utility undermining resource depletion tragedies. Ostrom then turned to assessing how to effectively manage these “common pool resources” (CPRs) which she posited were better addressed through local resource governing innovations. The point for coders here is that all of these efforts paid attention to the key features of the problem at hand – specifically those contextual and contingent factors that explained the particular utility undermining outcome for the problem at hand. Collective action theorists in general, and commons tragedy scholars in particular, only generalize based on the specific problem at hand, or a class of problems such as those commons tragedies in which excludability is not possible. They would never therefore apply their lessons to managing all problems nor bringing in an assessment as to whether the problem can be solved. They only look at the how to do so, not whether. Section 10: ### ii. Type 2: Utility Dominates, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) In contrast, **Type 2** literatures share an interest in understanding and championing utility seeking preferences, but they reject a focus that only includes attention to the specific problem at hand. The logic behind this, whether CBA that targets and aggregates individual preferences, or IR theories that target and aggregate state preferences, is that that group believes that it is unwise to assess policy options for ameliorating a specified problem without considering, and finding a way to incorporate, the result of this decision on other kinds of problems. Their logic is clear: if solving or effectively managing one specific kind of utility undermining activity – such as say, over harvesting timber – results in a set of policy and governance decisions that might preclude more lucrative activities – such as say the introduction of copper mining and its promise of increase wages – then policy officials have undermined their responsibilities in managing for the greatest amount of utility with which to benefit the welfare of citizens. This category also captures the IR scholars above who also seek to generalize about the processes through which states do, or should, maximizing their preferences – often in the name of the “national interest”- that are then applied to assess how – and sometimes even whether – the problem at hand can be effectively managed given state preferences. Hence those analysts that turn to state preferences as a way to think about how to manage and develop policy response – whether it be, as discussed below, Nordhaus’ reasoning that optimal state responses to the utility undermining impacts of the climate crisis will produce a 3.2 degree world – in contrast to the 1.5 outcome a problem contingent approach of the IPCC. It also applied, as discussed below, to neo-liberal institutionalist Keohane whose climate research turns to his theory of state preferences for policy solutions, rather than interrogating the contingent nature of the climate crisis itself. Hence whether CBA, or conflict resolution or a host of other likeminded approaches, Type 4 classifies those analytical approaches that are not offered as contingent on solving a particular problem but as a way to adjudicate the problem at hand by apply a universal way to think about, measure, and identify, and project policy interventions. Hence, these approach there expands consideration, and offering a framework for doing so, about whether the problem at hand **can, or should,** be effectively managed based on some kind of approach to preference formation. Section 11: ### iii. Type 3: Utility Does Not Dominate, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) There are many diverse approaches that are nonetheless united by their **Type 3 status** : i.e. analytical frames for understanding, explaining and prescribing policy actions that generate protocols for doing so that eschew utility motivations as a dominant force or outcome, and instead reflect on a diverse set of motivations with which to understand, and prescribe courses of action. These approaches are united by rejecting the treatment of non-utility outcomes as “irrational” and instead wonder how to understand and incorporate cultural values, social practices, environmental norms, that produce some kind of way to both understand the world and also provide the basis for recommendations about how to include a diverse set of ideas, organizations that generate legitimate process and wide accepted outcomes. As reviewed above when discussing universal approaches, while they reject utility as a dominant motivation, they share the idea that pre-existing theories and frameworks can be applied to adjudicate not only how, but whether, a particular problem can be solved. In other words, with these approaches – whether originating from a particular set of ethical frames in philosophy, sociology, geography or critical theory, one never knows for sure whether applying a particular one of these approaches to a particular problem will result in the problem at hand being solved or effectively managed. In other words, in contrast to the teleological orientation of Types 1 and 4, Type 3 approaches work from a preexisting framework – or develop a framework from a case that is meant to apply to any chase – rather than developing a framework that only pertains to that problem or class of problems. Section 12: ### iv. Type 4: Utility Does Not Dominate, Problem Contingent For these reasons **Type 4** frameworks are distinguished, like Type 1, on their ability to help understand, and ameliorate, a specified problem from the Paris Accord’s 1.5/2 degree objectives, to a particular species being threatened with extinction. However unlike Type 1 reinforcing approaches, Type 4 problems not only reject utility as the guiding force, they usually find that when those chasing utility outcomes are effective, they usually cause, or exacerbate, a range of Type 4 problems. The classic example comes to us from the identification of two distinct problems that are outside of the sustainability challenges to which Cashore devoted his empirical work. The first problem is that of slavery – i.e. the act of one human having property rights over another human being – i.e. “owning” them. A range of scholarly analyses assessing this problem have found that the only way to ensure slavery is dramatically reduced or eradicated is to begin, and end, with the required outcome: i.e. no one, for any reason, is allowed to own another person. Many apply relevant frameworks have not only found that (Type 1 and 2) utility chasing motivations was behind slavery in the first place, but that application of Type 3, 2 frames permits deliberations over whether slavery ought to be considered – which, by definition, undermines an ability to achieve the desired outcome. The second example is anti-colonial norms in which reducing or averting an attempt by one country to take over another can only be achieved by teleological approaches that beginning, and end, with the absence of colonialism. Scholarly analysis reinforcing Type 4 hence would reject neo-liberal and neo-realist IR theories as permitting an understanding of state preferences that could also consider colonial type behaviours, rather than assessing mechanisms for ensuring these possibilities are never considered. As I discuss below, a number of analytical frames within environmental studies are consistent with Type 4 reasoning. Section 13: ## f) Coding Challenges The above review will most certainly facilitate, and place in context, efforts to code qualitative documents against the 4PTs. This facilitates, rather than overcomes, several challenges that a code book must incorporate. First, while some documents may seem straightforward, others may provide a nuanced or even contradictory account that makes sorting more challenging. The solution to this is to provide, and justify, systematic protocols to facilitate sorting of what we refer to as “ambiguous” cases below. Second, different types of documents will need slightly different ways to disentangle the 4PTs. For example, our careful review of journal articles on sustainability policy and governance requires laborious coding of highly complex articles. Our original focused on the Journal of Regulation and Governance was used to both revise our 4PT distinctions, and then to go back, recode, and assess intercoder reliability. However, our approach to coding abstracts for course syllabi at Yale’s environment schools was much swifter and faster – as we only needed to see whether the course championed a utility outcome or not, and whether it was focused on applying its approach to specific on the ground problems. Both coding exercises permitted the development of dominant “key words” within each both deductively and inductively. This means that one solution for accelerating coding through quantitively analysis of text is to identify the prevalence of different key words associated with one problem type over the others. Of course this approach is not perfect because sometimes some concepts such as “cost effectiveness” analysis can be used to achieve any kind of Type 4, 3 2 and 1 outcomes, while “cost benefit” reinforces Type 2. It may be difficult for using a key word approach alone to therefore uncover these nuances. Hence, while this approach may not be as nuanced as the human coding orientation, it promises to provide more swift data that itself, will be useful. And as along as standardizes approach is taken, it will still produce useful data even if that set might be slightly different than if laborious human coders had applied our more nuanced approach. Finally, and to integrate these different efforts, our ultimate goal is to draw on AI techniques that might best replicate human coding, but in much less time. Another challenges concerns whether an article is simply categorized by asking and answer the two distinguishing questions, or, to save, time, whether a sorting approach can be undertaken through a “branching tree” metaphore: i.e. first ask: “utility” if so, (1 or 2) then ask “problem contingent?, if so 1 or 4. Under this approach Type 3 emerges because other types are excluded, rather than affirmatively coding for Type 3. The problem with this approach – and which tripped us up initially – was that Type 3 was more likely to include ambiguous cases and or cases beyond our universe (i.e. they did not actually pertain to policy and governance). As a result, we had to regroup and instead encourage coders to identify each one according to the two categories, and then to note cases where they simply could not figure out how to best apply the code book (which meant we needed to revise and provide better guidance) and/or be clearer about whether a paper was in or out of our universe. For example, we excluded purely science based classes from our review of Yale’s environment school, since most science based classes provided basic knowledge about problems – which might appear to be Type 4- but because they didn’t identify policy or governance solutions – where actually not part of our universe of analyses. **Article to Analyze:** Page 1: Bridging critical institutionalism and fragmented authoritarianism in China: An analysis of centralized water policies and their local implementation in semi-arid irrigation districts Raymond Yu Wang Center for Chinese Public Administration Research, School of Government, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou, China Tao Liu College of Urban and Environmental Sciences, Peking University, Beijing, China Center for Urban Future Research, Peking University, Beijing, China Heping Dang School of Humanities and Social Science, The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen, China Abstract This paper attempts to bridge critical institutionalism and fragmented authoritarianism in China. Moving beyond the predic- tive functionalist perspective and the overemphasis on state primacy, the paper focuses on how top-down water policies are exercised by local agencies, collective communities, and individuals at the county level and below. Through a case of irrigation management in the upper reaches of the Yellow River, this paper posits that institutional bricolage and fragmentation are prevalent despite the imposition of the authoritarian state. Moreover, it shows that local water policy implementation is reshaped by various contested discourse, the pursuit of different interests, social relations, and broad transformative forces. This high degree of diversity not only manifests an increasing institutional ﬂexibility, as water policies are operated outside China’s hierarchical bureaucratic system, but also is embedded in an era of socio-economic transformation where the pro- cesses of industrialization, urbanization, and neo-liberalization have changed the dynamics of rural governance. Keywords: China, critical institutionalism, fragmented authoritarianism, institutional bricolage, water governance. 1. Introduction In their article highlighting the scholarly trend of studying institutional bricolage and, more generally, critical institutionalism, Cleaver and de Koning wrote “There is a need to place local institutional arrangements within the wider frames of governance (the political ecology and political economy) which shape the possibilities for resource allocation, adaptation and negotiated solutions,” (Cleaver & de Koning 2015, p. 2). Their argument echoes the increasing attention paid by common-pool resource (CPR) scholars to the complexity and uncertainty nested in particular social-ecological environments (Clement 2010; Hall et al. 2014). The impacts of contextually sensitive features (i.e. social relations, politics of policies, community heterogeneities, power dynamics, and historical trajectories) on the performance of CPR governance have yet to be fully examined in diverse empirical settings, which therefore raises questions about whether the focus of CPR theory on efﬁciency and functionality is an oversimpliﬁcation of complex local conditions (Saunders 2014). For this line of inquiry, the broad governance context of China, which offers a number of unique socio- political and economic characteristics, has been insufﬁciently studied in the existing CPR literature. Traditionally, the Chinese political system is characterized by a number of authoritarian weaknesses, such as overcentralization Correspondence: Heping Dang, Room 313, Zhixin Building, School of Humanities and Social Science, CUHK(SZ), 2001 Long- xiang Road, Longgang District, Shenzhen 518172, China. Email: dangheping@cuhk.edu.cn Accepted for publication 4 May 2018. © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd Regulation & Governance (2018) doi:10.1111/rego.12198 Page 2: of decisionmaking, overreliance on coercive regulations, the prevalence of personal power, and the absence of the rule of law (Nathan 2003; Beeson 2010; Yan 2011). However, it would be overly simplistic to categorize China as an exemplar of authoritarianism and neglect the complex, multivariable, and multi-level institutional and social interplay nested in an increasingly diversiﬁed context (Lo 2015). As the pluralized dimensions of the Chinese society unfold, the notion of “fragmented authoritarianism” ﬁrst proposed by Lieberthal and Oksenberg (1988) has increasingly been used to analyze mixed and malleable policy outcomes shaped within an authoritarian state (Mertha 2009; Nickum 2010; Moore 2014a,b). In this sense, very few scholars have bridged fragmented authori- tarianism with critical institutionalism in their studies on institutional dynamics of local CPR governance. Cleaver and de Koning’s (2015) question of how institutions are animated by people in particular spaces has yet to be examined in the fragmented authoritarian political regime of China. Using water governance as an example, this paper stands at the intersection where China’s top-down political inputs interact with indigenous social-ecological and economic conditions to scrutinize the process of CPR gover- nance. This paper investigates how state-centric control over water is diversely exercised in rural areas where economic pursuits and social-historical relations at the lowest operational level inﬂect power relations. This approach does not evaluate the robustness of Ostrom’s (1990)design principles with selected cases (Cox et al. 2010); instead, it probes the diverse local practices and governance outcomes by moving beyond the predictive functionalist perspective. The operational roles of collective communes and individuals who exercise top-down policies with some degree of “localism” or “self-interest” are effectively included in the analytical scope of critical institutionalism and fragmented authoritarianism. Our objective is to conduct a local-level analysis within the broad political economy and explain a miscellaneous set of local water governance phenomena that emerges in the Chinese context. In the following sections, we ﬁrst review the relevant literature on critical institutionalism and fragmented authoritarianism. Next, we introduce a case study of water governance in semi-arid Northwest China. We then elaborate the diverse causes and processes of water policy implementation by probing into local irrigation practices that are entangled in the interplay between central policies, regional physical and social conditions, rural institutions, and politics at the village level. We conclude with a discussion on the implications of the micro- examination of critical institutionalism and fragmented authoritarianism in the Chinese context. 2. Literature review 2.1. Critical institutionalism Critical institutionalism is as much a critique of as a complement to mainstream institutionalism (Cleaver & de Koning 2015; Ingram et al. 2015). Mainstream institutional thinking, signiﬁed by the seminal and inﬂuential work of Ostrom (1990, 2009, 2010), holds a predictive perspective regarding institutional dynamics and collective action (Johnson 2004). Its underlying presumption is that favorable outcomes (i.e. sustainable CPR use) can be achieved through amendable institutions, which may not only share commonalities in nature but also may be crafted, shaped, and replicated in desirable directions and different scenarios (Hall et al. 2014). Mainstream insti- tutionalism has been the subject of a prominent line of inquiry for understanding and crafting long enduring arrangements for governing CPR in both academic and practitioner communities. Critical institutional thinking, although still nascent, often questions the rational choice assumption of the predictive perspective and queries the “fuzziness,” complexity, and uncertainty of diverse empirical settings where the explanatory power of main- stream institutionalism seems to be limited (Cleaver & de Koning 2015). One should note that critical institutionalism does not fully reject mainstream institutional thinking. In fact, their views may often overlap. For instance, both critical institutionalism and mainstream institutional thinking agree with the importance of institutions. Mainstream institutional scholars are also antagonistic to the idea of panacea and pay close attention to the context, scale, and interdependencies when applying Ostrom’s design prin- ciples (Ostrom et al. 2007; Baggio et al. 2016; Schlager 2016). Some empirical cases that are inconsistent with the predictions of the design principles have been identiﬁed and explained (Barnett et al. 2016). Yet the salient dis- tinction is that critical institutionalism is built upon an alternative assumption about the nature of human behav- ior. It emphasizes that the social, historical, and anthropological dimensions of human interactions are as important as the monitoring, incentives and sanctions that regulate how people individually or collectively use © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 2 R. Y. Wang et al. Institutionalism and authoritarianism Page 3: the commons. From a critical institutionalism perspective, the practical approaches that people adopt to manage CPR are not crafted or imported but are constantly modiﬁed, invented, and restructured, based on whatever resources are available, to adapt to changing challenges and new conﬁgurations of rules, as well as transforming norms and socio-economic relationships that are occurring in a particular governance regime. Moreover, these changing institutions could become functional and enduring as they draw legitimacy from accepted knowledge, culture, and established mechanisms of authority. Cleaver (2002, 2012) and de Koning (2014) summarize this dynamic process of ad hoc evolution and legitimization as “institutional bricolage.” The focus on “bricolage” substantially expands the scope of analysis on the commons. Critical institutional scholars are concerned about the multi-faceted and multi-scalar complexity of social interactions embedded in everyday life: micro-politics, historical formation, and biophysical geographies; therefore, they are skeptical about whether institutions can be mechanically crafted to be efﬁcient and whether institutional interventions, such as participation, empowerment, and social learning can be effectively translated into workable arrangements under complex empirical conditions. In this sense, critical institutionalism embraces a larger pool of literature than mainstream institutionalism. The focus of critical institutionalism regarding the transformation of institutions, the operationalization of institutions by human beings, and the relationship between institutional form and out- comes requires a comprehensive examination of institutional dynamics and their compatibility with local condi- tions (Leong & Lejano 2016). The impacts of broader governance on local institutions are one of the key themes of critical institutionalism (Cleaver 2012; Cleaver & de Koning 2015). This line of inquiry could draw insights from political economy and political ecology analysis, which focuses on unequal and dynamic power relations as well as their roles in shaping institutional arrangements for CPR governance at the local level (Blaikie 2006; Andersson & Ostrom 2008; Jones 2015). As a fragmented authoritarian regime under rapid socio-economic transformation, China offers a valuable case for assessing the process of “institutional bricolage.” With very little research placing critical institutionalism in the broader political context of China, this paper aims at engaging in a discussion with the CPR literature through a Chinese case of local irrigation governance arrangements, which are developed in the dynamic mixture of externally imposed institutional crafting, as well as socially and historically embedded interactions. 2.2. Fragmented authoritarianism As a widely used and durable analytical framework for understanding Chinese politics in the open and reform era, fragmented authoritarianism captures the key features of the evolution of the Chinese political process, during which the state’s dominance has been gradually eroded by the pluralization of civil society and the development of the market, while the state’s power remains strong enough to maintain its political and socio- economic agenda against demands from non-state actors (Mertha 2009). In the ﬁeld of environmental governance, two main streams of scholarship have been developed under the framework of fragmented authori- tarianism. One stream focuses on policymaking processes during which previously marginalized actors, such as non-governmental organizations (NGOs), the media, and individuals, seek to, or even manage to, insert their concerns into centralized policies (Mertha 2009; Grabosky 2013; van Rooij et al. 2016). The other stream focuses on policy implementation processes where problems of opportunistic behavior, poor coordination, and weak enforcement capacity in local states are prevalent (Feng et al. 2006; Marks 2010; Ran 2013). In this sense, “frag- mentation” could be a trend toward pluralization – the incremental inclusion of diverse non-state actors in the policymaking process, in addition to a closed circle of party and state ofﬁcials, seems to be a positive response to diverse demands from society (Lema & Ruby 2007; Zhang et al. 2013a; Shen 2014). Additionally, “fragmentation” could be formed by cross-level and cross-sectoral bargaining and variant operational contexts in which the diverse political goals of the vertical strands of functional agencies (tiao) and the horizontal threads of territorially based administrative units (kuai) are mixed and reshaped, leading to weak enforcement and even the failure of centralized policies at the subnational level (Marks 2010; Kostka 2016). After a careful review of the current literature on the entry of “new actors” in policymaking processes and the prevalence of “command-without-control” in policy implementation, a salient gap has emerged. This gap points to an inadequate examination of the roles of local practitioners, which mainly include county governments and lower level agencies, as well as rural communities and peasants, who exercise many of the top-down policies. In © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 3 Institutionalism and authoritarianism R. Y. Wang et al. Page 4: other words, it is problematic to study China’s cross-scale political interplay within only the bureaucratic system or simply from a mechanical perspective. Given the growing complexity of the social reality, one should recognize that a full circle policy process involves continuous interactions among different groups of actors whose divergent interests, strategies, and reasoning are ineluctably intertwined from policy formulation to policy operation. As Rawski notes, “ofﬁcial policy is only part of the reform story (Rawski 1995, p. 1169).” It is no longer news that local states do not passively accept everything externally imposed on them, and they do not fully control how policy outcomes are translated in empirical settings (Olivier de Sardan 1988). In this sense, local states are not “a cog in the machine” that only enforce state-centric decisions at the end of the policy process; rather, they could be a starting point at which new rules might be developed in speciﬁc local contexts and diversely operated by non-state actors (i.e. peasants) in their daily lives. 2.3. Bridging critical institutionalism and fragmented authoritarianism Although derived from different ﬁelds of study, critical institutionalism and fragmented authoritarianism con- verge at dynamic local spaces where complex, uncertain, and evolving networks of social relations, micro-politics, and economic transformation capture the attention of both lines of scholarship. Through the lens of irrigation management in a Chinese context, the integration of critical institutionalism and fragmented authoritarianism raises three strands of key questions that warrant further examination. First, can centralized water policies initiated by the state at the national level be effectively enforced at the local level? To what extent do the practices derived from these policies represent the state’s aspiration for water governance reform? Is there evidence of “bricolage” that reshapes policy implementation? This strand of ques- tions probes into the interfaces between authoritative power and its exercise, between national discourse and rural values, and between regulation and practice. It is of great importance to examine the translation of power across the hierarchical order and the occurrence of institutional changes that characterize the fragmentation of China’s authoritarian political system (Yang 2013; Lo 2015; van Rooij et al. 2016. Likewise, critical institutional scholars also often focus on these interfaces where bricolage emerges to navigate discrepant logic, knowledge, values, and pursuits of interests (Cleaver & de Koning 2015; Funder & Marani 2015; Marin & Bjorklund 2015). Second, to what extent do imported institutions, which represent examples of “best practices,” ﬁt with local con- ditions? How can we understand the emergence of locally driven rules-in-use through a process of institutional bri- colage? Against the external imposition of institutional crafting, can innovations arise from adapting particular contexts and improvising on existing practices? For institutional scholars, the concept of institutional ﬁt is intui- tively appealing, as it associates crafted institutions with various dimensions of the social-ecological system with which they interact (Young 2002; Folke et al. 2007; Epstein et al. 2015). However, institutional ﬁt is also a relatively vague concept that leaves unanswered questions in regard to the deﬁning features of ﬁt and the typology of institu- tional arrangements for facilitating ﬁt across scales and levels (Cox 2008; Vatn & Vedeld 2012). The complexity and uncertainty embedded in different social-ecological systems make it difﬁcult to apply “best practices” in a con- sistent way. In this sense, the growing discussion about adaption, resilience, social learning, and vulnerability sug- gests an alternative perspective of “ﬁt” (Pahl-Wostl et al. 2007; Carpenter et al. 2009; Young 2010; Folke et al. 2010, 2016). This alternative perspective essentially acknowledges the generality of “misﬁt” and calls for a realistic approach rather than an idealistic attempt to import “best practices” for governing CPRs (Keskitalo et al. 2016). The alternative view is not only consistent with emphasis on the sensitivity and legitimacy mirrored in institutional bricolage but also provokes discussion of the literature on fragmented authoritarianism in China, as it concerns the robustness of an authoritarian state and the sources of its authoritarian resilience under both internal and external challenges (Perry 2007; Cai 2008; Schubert 2008; Yan 2011). The frameworks of responsive authoritarianism (Grabosky 2013; Chen et al. 2016) and consultative authoritarianism (He & Thogersen 2010; Teets 2013) have been developed to analyze policy innovations, institutional reforms, and polycentricism in China, which are presumed to be excluded from authoritarian regimes. The dynamics of fragmented authoritarianism, along with the emergence of local institutions during the process of bricolage, jointly offer a context where new organizational forms of insti- tutions can be studied. Finally, what are the impacts of socio-economic and demographic changes on local institutional arrange- ments? How are these changes associated with the rapidly evolving Chinese political economy? Unlike previous © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 4 R. Y. Wang et al. Institutionalism and authoritarianism Page 5: focus on the translation of power and the emergence of local institutions, the last strand of questions places extra attention on societal changes in rural communities during China’s rapid economic development. This is a direct response to Cleaver and de Koning’s (2015) argument of the role of a broader political economy in shaping insti- tutions. In particular, the massive scale and breakneck speed of economic development, which is led by the authoritarian state and facilitated by a pluralizing private sector and civil society, have substantially altered the socio-economic structure of Chinese rural communities. Rural–urban migration, urban land expansion, diversiﬁ- cation of agriculture, and improvement of productivity have characterized a dynamic environment of institutional changes. In contrast to a relatively static environment in Southeast Asia and Latin America, where many previous studies of irrigation management have been conducted, the transforming Chinese political economy contains a unique “recipe” for vigorous bricolage and fragmented authoritarianism that have yet to be fully investigated by institutional scholars. 3. Case and method Water control has been instrumental in pushing forward the state’s political, economic, and social agendas in China. From the Maoist era, when agricultural hydraulic constructions were associated with the establishment of rural collectives and the mobilization of rural labor (Perkins 1969; Li & Yang 2005, to the open and reform era, when the Chinese state carried out a number of large-scale water projects (i.e. Three Gorges Dam and the South- to-North Water Diversion Project) to maintain rapid economic development (Webber et al. 2017), the dispersed practices of water governance in China have been inescapably political both in and beyond the state (Agnew 2011; Rogers et al. 2016). Water governance comprises interactions not only within the bureaucratic system at various levels but also among different sectors of society, where enterprises, activists, NGOs, the media, and indi- viduals are more or less involved in the process. These complexities of China’s water governance system make it a suitable case for a study of how institutional bricolage and fragmented authoritarianism have evolved in an increasingly diversiﬁed context. The Ningxia Hui Autonomous Region (Ningxia) is selected as a case study to facilitate a detailed elaboration of the dynamics of bricolage and fragmentation nested in China’s water governance system. Because Ningxia is located in a semi-arid region, it contains a broadening gap between water supply and demand, which vividly illustrates China’s nationwide water scarcity problem. With the Yellow River ﬂowing across its territory, Ningxia also encompasses a sophisticated irrigation system with a long agricultural history and a number of innovative water institutions. The variations in irrigation and agricultural activities could offer a valuable opportunity and rich material to examine how water governance is exercised at the local level. Most of our ﬁeldwork was carried out in two county-level regions, namely, Qingtongxia (QTX) and Hongsibu (HSB). We conducted ﬁeldwork in eight villages located within QTX and HSB to facilitate a detailed examination of the local operation of water governance (Fig. 1). Although these case study sites are all located within the Wuzhong municipality, they demonstrate distinctive features of irrigation. QTX is close to water sources, is located in a ﬂat ﬂood plain, and adopts a traditional irrigation system in which surface water is distributed by gravity. However, the gravity irrigation system cannot reach HSB, which is located approximately 50 km away from the mainstream of the Yellow River and 100 m above the surface water level of the nearest main irrigation channel. A series of pumping stations and channels have been built since the 1990s as part of a poverty allevia- tion project to artiﬁcially lift water to HSB, one of China’s most impoverished areas. These divided irrigation methods are an adaptation to different geophysical conditions and have been formed by heterogeneous social and agricultural contexts. Data are mainly drawn from semi-structured interviews and group discussions that were conducted in four ﬁeld trips to Ningxia from September 2014 to January 2016. Each ﬁeld trip lasted approximately two weeks. During these ﬁeld trips, we ﬁrst approached the Water Resources Department of the Ningxia provincial govern- ment, where group discussions were held with seven government ofﬁcials with intimate knowledge of Ningxia’s water governance issues. Various topics ranging from water consumption control to institutional innovation (e.g. water rights transfer) were discussed. At our request, we were then introduced to the Water Bureaus of the QTX and HSB governments, where we conducted individual interviews with six ofﬁcials directly involved with the implementation of top-down water policies at the county level and below. With their help, we interviewed © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 5 Institutionalism and authoritarianism R. Y. Wang et al. Page 6: each and every chairman of the local Water User Associations (WUAs) in the eight case study villages, which were selected to cover different biophysical conditions in both gravity and lift irrigation districts. In addition, we hosted group discussions in each village with representatives of the village committee, who are core members of the WUAs. The topics of the interviews and group discussions at the village level included basic demographic and socio-economic characteristics of the village, land use and agricultural activities, irrigation arrangements, water conﬂict and resolution mechanisms, external policies and their local enforcement, village history, social relations, and so forth. We also collected information regarding these topics through two random household visits in each village, as well as through interviews with three scholars from the Ningxia Academy of Agricultural Science who had previous ﬁeldwork experience in these villages. The results corroborated what we learnt from the interviews and group discussions.
