In addition, this qualitative study does not aim to establish causal relations between variables based on statistical models, but to explore complexity and diversity in institutions. Therefore, it does not need a representative sample of respondents, but those who are qualiﬁed to present variety in experi- ence, beliefs, and opinions about irrigation institutions and rural governance. We believe our research methods have served this purpose. Secondary data, such as ofﬁcial documents, project reports, and academic publications, were also used as supplementary sources to assist the analysis. 4. Analysis Previous research on China’s hydro-politics and policy implementation has primarily centered on central-local relations within the formal political system, such as compliance with mandates, cadre management and assess- ment, and corruption (O’Brien & Li 1999; Cai 2004; Hillman 2010; Smith 2010; Zhou 2010; Ahlers & Schubert 2015). Alternatively, we focus on the prosaic dimensions of the policy implementation process and the roles of rural communities that eventually implement such policies and incorporate them into their everyday lives, despite the fact they are not formal actors. With the objective of examining the process of “bricolage” and “fragmenta- tion” through the lens of irrigation management, we reveal the dynamics of institutional changes from three aspects, namely, the exercise of the state’s power, the import of public participatory organizations and the emer- gence of local institutions, and the impacts of socio-economic changes. We approach this task by describing the irrigation management practices and local socio-economic activities in two groups of villages located in the gravity irrigation districts of QTX and the lifting irrigation districts of HSB. 4.1. The exercise of power Water governance in China demonstrates prominent features of command-and-control, state-centric planning, and technocracy (Wang et al. 2017). This governance system is based upon the 1982 Constitution and the 2002 Water Law. The former established the public ownership of all natural resources, including water (Article 9), and the latter established an overall framework for both river basin agencies and territorially-based administrative agencies to oversee various water governance issues, including water planning (Article 17), water allocation (Article 44), gross volume control, and ration management (Article 47). Under this overarching governance framework, the Chinese state seems to be the sole dominant actor in the arena of water governance. We have identiﬁed three salient approaches regarding the state’s control of water in our case study area. First, the Yellow River water allocation plan, which is imposed by the state on lower-level jurisdictions (including Ningxia) through top-down administrative orders, divides limited water resources among different jurisdictions, sectors, or industries (State Council 1987; Shen & Speed 2009). In principle, each water user must acquire a water extraction permit and is not allowed to exceed their water-withdraw allotment. Second, the state’s control of water consumption is further enhanced by its monopoly of the water supply. In the irrigation districts of QTX and HSB, the peasants, as water users, must purchase water from the suppliers who primarily include channel management agencies and pumping stations. Both water suppliers are public institutions afﬁliated with the Water Resources Department of the provincial government. Their operation is therefore subject to scrutiny by their superordinate, whose primary responsibilities include keeping regional water consumption within the allocated quotas. Last but not least, the promotion of new technologies is another main policy instrument for reg- ulating water consumption. In addition to the top-down imposed regulations, the state has provided funding sup- port for a water-saving infrastructure for peasants, with the objective of facilitating local adaptation to stringent water conditions. However, it is overly simplistic to conclude that these state-centric arrangements can effectively prevent the overexploitation of water resources. Authoritarian power seems to be dwindling in the process of policy imple- mentation at the micro level. Particularly because a top priority in cadre assessments is to maintain social stability (Lee & Zhang 2013), the local government deﬁnitely does not want to see collective rural resistance caused by cutting off the water supply to peasants. An ofﬁcial from the Provincial Water Resources Department stated: © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 7 Institutionalism and authoritarianism R. Y. Wang et al. Page 8: We have talked about demand management for quite a long time, but in practice, our main tasks remain to ensure water supply. Neither the government nor the peasants can afford the losses from water shortages.2 In fact, none of the eight villages we visited had experienced water suspensions resulting from constraints on the water allotment; on the contrary, when shortage problems occur during busy times of the year or dry years, ad hoc arrangements for provisional water supply, such as temporary water diversion and the allocation of con- tingent allotments, are usually made to ensure agricultural production and food security. As a consequence, the “red line” of water consumption seems crossable when it mingles with other governance issues. This seemingly improvised result, from the bricolage perspective, revealed a hidden logic of institutions; namely, water consump- tion control is yet to be fully accepted in practice as it is contested by dominating discourses of social stability and livelihood improvement. Second, the water suppliers, as public institutions, were reluctant to reduce water consumption because they receive signiﬁcant income from selling water to the peasants. The staff salaries of channel management agencies were not fully supported by budgetary appropriations until 2013, so the collected water fees had served as extra- budgetary income to support the agencies’ operation.3 Under this arrangement, corruption and opportunistic behavior occurred among the water suppliers. A few peasants from HSB revealed: Last year, our villagers had a ﬁght with the people from the pumping station. Our village is located at the end of the channel, so it is difﬁcult for us to get enough water. But we found out that the pumping station leased out a piece of land nearby to an outside investor and secretly sold water to the investor. They made individual proﬁts from public services, and it cost us badly.4 In this sense, the state’s control of the water supply is not necessarily effective in curtailing water consump- tion, although the water suppliers are supposed to fulﬁll their public duties by enforcing governmental regulations. Finally, the promotion of the water-saving infrastructure has also encountered some difﬁculties because of the lack of social support. Some peasants are skeptical about the effectiveness of drip irrigation, as it contradicts the conventional wisdom of “the more water there is, the better crop yield.” One of the local peasants expressed his concern: I know this is technology, but there must be some impact[s] if less water ﬂows into my farmland. Would the government compensate my losses if I could not produce as much as I did before?5 Other peasants have concerns regarding the continuity of ﬁnancial support. The current policy for supporting the water-saving infrastructure is a temporary approach. There is no clear timeline in regard to how long the support will last. The peasants are reluctant, as they do not wish to bear the costs if ﬁscal transfer from the central government stops. This analysis shows that seemingly strong water governance institutions are reshaped by contested policy discourses, opportunism, and the lack of social understanding of new technologies, demonstrating that there is evidence of fragmentation and bricolage during the process of power translation when the authoritarian state’s water control policies are diversely exercised in rural communities. 4.2. The import of Water User Associations (WUAs) and the emergence of local institutions In addition to the domestic water institutions imposed by the authoritarian state, externally imported institutions have been crafted in our case study area. WUAs, which are considered an effective public participatory approach for maintaining the sustainable use of water resources (Wang et al. 2007; Zhang et al. 2013b; Hu et al. 2014; Huang 2014), have been widely established since the early 2000s in conjunction with World Bank projects at the requirement of the Ningxia government (Wang et al. 2010). Each of our case study villages has a WUA, which is mainly responsible for managing various local water affairs, such as the collection of water fees, the organization of irrigation activities, and the mobilization of collective action for maintaining irrigation facilities. However, the efﬁcacy of the WUAs is unexceptional. In practice, we found a substantial organizational and functional overlap between the WUAs and the village committees; the latter is the main village organization for attending to public affairs in rural China. Thus, the © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 8 R. Y. Wang et al. Institutionalism and authoritarianism Page 9: establishment of WUAs does not change the underlying structure of village governance. The reason is that water issues are not always about water per se; irrigation management often involves the coordination of collective action, the reconciliation of tensions, and the balancing of interests among villagers. This situation means that water issues cannot be isolated from other collective matters, which often fall outside the jurisdiction of the WUAs. According to a WUA chairman: A villager might refuse to pay water fees because he felt that he was unfairly treated last year when the village appropriated a piece of his land for road construction. He demands more compensation; otherwise he [will] not pay the water fees.6 This type of resistant action is not a rejection of the concept of “paying water fees as a duty” but a strategy to pursue his belief of fairness in a rural community where various collective affairs are interconnected and thus more complicated than water per se. Furthermore, the operation of the WUAs is exceptionally challenging in a closely connected Chinese rural society where “everyone knows everyone.” The enforcement of rules and sanctions is challenged by blurry bound- aries between “right” and “wrong.” For instance, it would seem callous, and practically unfeasible, to punish those who violate rules, such as those who pay their water fees late, those who commit petty water thefts, or those who are “free-riders.” Sanctioning is even more impracticable when the majority of the violators did not intentionally break the rules. Their violations or failures might be attributed to other socio-economic reasons, such as large medical expenditures, delayed salary payments, or the loss of the ability to work as a result of injury or sickness. Under these conditions, strict punishments (i.e. water supply cutoff ) would have caused greater economic losses to already vulnerable households. In practice, bending the rules to accommodate unique situations in rural com- munities is not considered “corruption;” rather, both the WUAs and water suppliers have to adjust, reshape, and even improvise the rules so that they are more compatible with the multifarious local social conditions. In other words, many functions of WUAs have not been legitimized in the rural Chinese context. The sanctioning rules, as externally imported institutions, are contested and even rejected by a number of endogenous social norms, which accommodate the sense of reciprocity and kindness rather than rigid institutional enforcement at the com- munity level. Although the impacts of WUAs seem limited, some locally initiated institutional innovations have emerged in our case study area. Water rights reform is a salient example. China has a complex water rights system as a result of a mix of ownership (e.g. collective-owned and state-owned), a mismatch between water suppliers (those who withdraw from water bodies), and water appropriators (those who consume water resources), as well as instable water availability in terms of both quantity and quality. In general, state ownership of water resources is exercised by the State Council and delegated to different subnational governmental agencies through water allocation plans. The right to withdraw water is given to a variety of water suppliers (i.e. pumping stations) who obtain permits from water agencies.7 The right to utilize water is enjoyed by a large number of stakeholders, such as irrigation districts, communes, peasants, urban residents, and manufacturing industries who purchase water from water suppliers. In this complex system, local attempts to initiate new irrigation rules often involve innovative interpretations or applications of unclearly deﬁned water rights. For instance, Yanchi County, another county located in Wuzhong municipality, redistributes all of its water allotment all the way down to the household level. The household irrigation allotments are delineated according to the area of farmland and farming pattern, which essentially combines the water use right with the land use right. Each household’s allotment is documented by a water ticket system issued by the local water management agency. The peasants have to present a water ticket each time they need water, but the ticket cannot be traded. This is an approach that reallocates public water rights outside of the administrative matrix, which demonstrates private characteristics but has yet to be clariﬁed by the legal framework. In HSB, an online platform through which water use allotments can be traded was estab- lished within the county. This platform is only accessible to a small number of main water users, but it is a more audacious attempt at water reallocation. Unlike the water ticket system, which only gives rural households “the right to use” the water, the platform allows the “right to beneﬁt” from the water, which represents a higher level of private right and thus requires more legal and political scrutiny in the socialistic regime. © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 9 Institutionalism and authoritarianism R. Y. Wang et al. Page 10: Although there is a limit to the scope and efﬁcacy of these new policies, they represent a local solution to local problems. These policies were neither imposed by the state nor imported from “best practices” but emerged from empirical local needs. This phenomenon demonstrates strong Chinese characteristics of policy innovation, which draws upon experiences of problem solving at the local level (Heilmann 2008). The “grey area,” where unclariﬁed property rights and unorthodox practices are allowed, offers an opportunity for different interpretations of insti- tutions, and thus may contribute to the fragmentation of policy outcomes in local practices. A local ofﬁcial advised: Our allotment is not enough, but we need to develop. We are a poor county in a developing country. People want to live a better life by farming more and making more money. My superiors, on the other hand, need to enforce the gross volume control target. Therefore, we must ﬁgure something out to accomplish both. We use the water tickets to let the peasants know that water is not enough, but we also try to ask for more water use quotas for more development.8 Similarly, a local ofﬁcial from the agricultural department shared his opinion as to why institutions cannot be mechanically implemented or imported in rural areas: The peasants are very simple. Their concerns are mostly about money. However, the peasants are also compli- cated. There is a complex social network that cannot be easily changed or understood by outsiders.9 4.3. The impacts of socio-economic changes Although Chinese rural society maintains a strong sense of community and social cohesion as well as unique local characteristics, it has been rapidly transforming since the 1980s (Guo 2001). The implementation of the household responsibility system has substantially increased agricultural productivity and freed hundreds of mil- lions of rural laborers (Krusekopf 2002). China’s continuous economic reforms have proceeded at a rapid pace toward urbanization and modernization, which have contributed greatly to rural–urban migration and structural changes in rural governance (Chan & Zhang 1999; Liu & Murphy 2006). The socio-economic changes associated with China’s rapid development have led to signiﬁcant impacts on irrigation institutions. For instance, we witnessed a trend toward the marginalization of irrigation in rural affairs. A WUA chairman advised that it was very difﬁcult to ﬁnd villagers willing to work in the WUA: It is simple math. A young man could make at least 100 Yuan a day working construction or manufacturing jobs in the city nearby, but working as a WUA staff member pays only slightly more than 1,000 Yuan a month. Sometimes when we cannot collect water fees from the villagers, we have to use our own money to ﬁll in the hole.10 In this sense, the lack of ﬁnancial incentive also explains the functional overlap between the WUAs and vil- lage committees. With young and capable labor pursuing a better life in the cities, village cadres have to remain as chairmen and members of the WUAs who attend to irrigation and other collective affairs together. In addition to the marginalization of irrigation, changes to land policy, the agricultural paradigm, and their induced social-political problems have also inﬂuenced the irrigation method (i.e. applying a water-saving infra- structure) and the change in relevant water institutions. A salient example is the promotion of land transfer and modern agriculture. At the national level, this is a policy response to increasing rural–urban migration and rela- tively low agricultural efﬁciency caused by farmland fragmentation.11 The policy allows peasants to transfer their land-use rights (gained from the collective) to other farmers or economic entities (Ye 2015). In other words, the migrants, who mostly work in the cities instead of on farmland, could lease out their contracted land to large land holders, such as family farms, peasant cooperatives, and even investors. As a result, fragmented farmland plots can be integrated, which promotes agricultural commoditization, specialization, and modernization, including the establishment of a modern water-saving infrastructure. However, scaling up farmland is not possible unless a reasonably large number of peasants with adjacent farmland all agree to the transfer. Many social conﬂicts arise during the bargaining and operation of land transfers (Gong & Zhang 2017), which then impede the application of water-saving infrastructure that requires relatively large-scale agricultural production. In this sense, the barriers © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 10 R. Y. Wang et al. Institutionalism and authoritarianism Page 11: to effective irrigation management are not directly water-related; they could instead be a result of socio-economic changes. Socio-economic development in China also brings about changes to peasants’ attitudes about agricultural activities and irrigation. With fading differences between urban and rural lifestyles and increasing living standards for peasants, the extra work associated with the maintenance of new irrigation infrastructure now seems burden- some. A local peasant told us: Drip irrigation is a good thing. I could control the amount of water. It is good for my grape farm and saves water. A few years ago, the government gave us money to build a small water tank for drip irrigation. Many of us tried but eventually gave up. The Yellow River water contains too much silt, it clogs the dripping pipes, and we must clear the sediment in the water tank regularly. This is too much trouble. Additionally, we do not want to be tied here to the farmland. Now the water tank is abandoned.12 We cannot assert that the failure of the drip irrigation case is typical. However, we argue that new collective action and social problems related to irrigation institutions stem from socio-economic changes in the broader Chinese economy. The outcome of water governance in a particular region could be inﬂuenced by the changes in agricultural patterns and population, the expansion of farmland area, the transfer of farmland and water rights, and the price ﬂuctuation of agricultural products, which warrant further examination in future studies. 5. Discussion and conclusions Water governance in China has rapidly and profoundly changed over the past decade. This process has been guided by the state’s aspiration for maintaining sustainable water use, improving water use efﬁciency and optimizing water distribution among regions, sectors, and individual users. Meanwhile, we have witnessed a variety of local actions resulting in fragmentation and bricolage in this seemingly authoritarian reform. The combination of uniﬁcation and decentralization, state-centrism and localism, and top-down and bottom-up jointly makes water governance an interesting case for the study of critical intuitionalism and Chinese politics. Using water governance in the Ningxia irrigation districts as a case study, this paper engages in a discussion of critical institutionalism and fragmented authoritarianism with empirical evidence encapsulated in three dimen- sions, namely, the exercise of power in controlling water, the import of the WUAs, and the emergence of local water rights institutions, as well as the impacts of socio-economic changes on collective action. First, we have shown that the power of the state in controlling the water supply has been reshaped during its translation by con- tested policy discourses, the pursuit of different interests, varying social norms, and diverse relations in rural con- text. Rather than focusing on the principal agent problem between central and local states, we have demonstrated that the exercise of authoritarian power is fragmented outside the bureaucratic system where diverse social- political and historical elements play a key role in inﬂuencing policy outcomes. Second, the insights generated from the examination of “fragmentation” in an authoritarian context demonstrate that the process of “institu- tional bricolage” is prevalent in irrigation practices. Not only do local actors adapt to the state’s control of total water consumption, their actions against externally imposed policies have also been legitimized by established social norms. The persistent function of village committees indicates that local irrigation practices are still domi- nated by social-historical relations rather than imported institutions such as WUAs. Moreover, problem-solving- oriented local practices even stimulated diverse institutional innovations (i.e. water rights reform), which simulta- neously respond to the state’s objective of water consumption control and the farmers’ desire for beneﬁt maximi- zation. Last but not least, the socio-economic changes associated with the macro-level political economy of China have substantially impacted local irrigation practices and institutions. The rapid urbanization and modernization processes have led to changing demographic and social conditions in rural communities. These changes, in turn, raised new collective action and social problems that enhance fragmentation and bricolage.
